text: In this video, we demonstrate a top-down approach for robotic adrenalectomy with dissection along specific anatomic boundaries. We will start on the right. Our patient is a fifty-six year old woman with a three point six centimeter right incidentally found adrenal mass that increased in size to four point one centimeters. Past medical history, surgical history, and medications are listed. Lab work was suggestive of a cortisol-producing tumor. Shown here are axial and coronal images of a preoperative CT scan.

The patient is positioned in a right side up lateral position. Robotic ports are placed for access. The right adrenal mass is visible caudal to the liver. The liver is dissected to expose the diaphragm superior to the adrenal gland, and a grasper inserted through a 5mm trocar in the epigastrium retracts the liver cranially.

The surgeon exposes the inferior vena cava and right adrenal vein, placing a surgical clip on the adrenal vein before cauterizing it laterally with bipolar energy and dividing it. The adrenal gland is dissected superiorly, revealing the diaphragm, while arteries supplying the gland medially are cauterized and incised. The gland is released medially from the inferior vena cava using cautery and sharp dissection, proceeding just superior to the renal hilum. Dissection continues on the upper pole of the kidney, avoiding branches of the renal vessels. The adrenal gland, mass, and surrounding periadrenal fat are removed entirely, and the adrenalectomy bed is inspected for hemostasis.
The surgeon mobilizes the spleen by dividing its peritoneal reflections, followed by further mobilization of the spleen and pancreas to expose the diaphragm above the left adrenal gland.

The surgeon carefully safeguards the splenic vessels and pancreas while identifying and isolating the left adrenal vein. The vein is cauterized using bipolar energy, followed by placement of a surgical clip and division of the vein. Medial dissection is performed using bipolar diathermy, dividing small vessels lateral to the cruce of the diaphragm.

The surgeon performs inferior dissection cranial to the renal vein and artery, followed by lateral dissection on the surface of the upper pole of the kidney. The final surrounding attachments are removed, and the adrenal gland is extracted completely. The adrenalectomy bed is inspected for hemostasis.
The gross pathology specimen is displayed, showing the aldosteronoma above and normal adrenal tissue below in a closer view of the adrenal tumor.
There is no significant differences in clinical outcomes, such as operative time, estimated blood loss, length of hospital stay, complication rate, and readmission rate. Thank you.
